title: Occupational Therapist
metaTitle: Occupational Therapist Resume: Examples & Writing Tips
metaDescription: Build a strong OT resume with real examples, key skills, and hiring manager insights. Land interviews faster with our actionable resume guide.
h1: How to Write an Occupational Therapist Resume That Gets Interviews
intro: Your occupational therapy resume needs to show both clinical expertise and measurable patient outcomes—not just a list of credentials. We'll walk you through the specific skills, formats, and language that hiring managers at hospitals, clinics, schools, and rehab centers actually respond to.
audience: Recent OT graduates, newly licensed occupational therapists, and experienced OTs looking to transition between settings (clinical to school, acute to outpatient, etc.).

topSkills.name: Patient Assessment & Functional Evaluation
topSkills.why: Hiring managers need to see you can independently evaluate patients' occupational needs and limitations—it's the foundation of all OT work.

topSkills.name: Treatment Planning & Intervention Design
topSkills.why: Employers want evidence you can develop individualized therapy plans that address specific client goals, not generic templates.

topSkills.name: ADL & IADL Training
topSkills.why: Activities of daily living and instrumental ADL training are core OT responsibilities; showing mastery here proves competency in the role's essence.

topSkills.name: Electronic Health Records (EHR) & Documentation
topSkills.why: Clinical documentation is non-negotiable; familiarity with specific EHR systems (Epic, Cerner, etc.) makes you immediately deployable.

topSkills.name: Manual Therapy & Therapeutic Exercises
topSkills.why: Hands-on clinical skills—range of motion, strengthening, joint protection—differentiate you from candidates with only book knowledge.

topSkills.name: Sensory Integration & Pediatric Therapy
topSkills.why: If you work with children, sensory processing frameworks and pediatric assessment tools are highly valued by school districts and pediatric clinics.

topSkills.name: Mental Health & Psychosocial Interventions
topSkills.why: Many OT settings now emphasize mental health and cognition; demonstrating competency here opens doors to psychiatry, community mental health, and wellness roles.

topSkills.name: Interdisciplinary Collaboration & Case Conferencing
topSkills.why: OTs work across teams (PT, speech, nursing, social work); showing you communicate and coordinate care signals you're ready for real clinical environments.

topSkills.name: Assistive Technology & Adaptive Equipment
topSkills.why: Prescribing and training patients on mobility aids, home modifications, and adaptive devices is increasingly expected even at entry level.

topSkills.name: HIPAA Compliance & Safety Protocols
topSkills.why: Healthcare employers screen resumes for evidence of ethical practice and awareness of patient privacy—explicitly mention it.

sampleBullets.weak: Provided occupational therapy treatment to patients with various diagnoses and discharged them when appropriate.
sampleBullets.strong: Completed occupational therapy assessments for 40+ inpatients with stroke, orthopedic, and neurological diagnoses; designed and implemented individualized treatment plans that resulted in 75% of patients meeting self-care and mobility goals within 2–4 weeks of admission.
sampleBullets.lesson: Specific diagnosis types, patient volume, and outcome percentages turn a vague responsibility into proof of clinical impact.

sampleBullets.weak: Worked with the interdisciplinary team to support patient outcomes.
sampleBullets.strong: Collaborated with physical therapy, nursing, and social work in weekly case conferences; communicated discharge recommendations and home safety modifications that prevented 3 hospital readmissions in a 3-month period.
sampleBullets.lesson: Naming specific team members and linking collaboration to a measurable outcome (readmission reduction) shows you understand the value of teamwork.

sampleBullets.weak: Trained patients and caregivers on adaptive equipment and home modifications.
sampleBullets.strong: Educated 50+ patients and family members on proper use of walkers, reachers, dressing aids, and bathroom safety equipment; documented training completion in EHR; 90% of caregivers reported confidence in providing assistance post-discharge.
sampleBullets.lesson: Patient volume, documentation method, and caregiver confidence metric prove both productivity and quality of education.

commonMistakes.fix: Write 'Licensed Occupational Therapist (OTR/L), [State], License #[if relevant]' to confirm active licensure and state—employers need to know you're legally cleared to practice.

commonMistakes.mistake: Focusing on duties instead of outcomes (e.g., 'conducted range-of-motion exercises')
commonMistakes.fix: Always tie clinical tasks to patient or organizational results: 'Conducted ROM exercises that improved shoulder abduction by 30 degrees, enabling patient to return to light assembly work.'

commonMistakes.mistake: Not mentioning specific settings or client populations
commonMistakes.fix: State the exact setting (acute-care hospital, outpatient clinic, school, skilled nursing facility) and populations you've worked with—hiring managers want to know if you fit their environment.

commonMistakes.mistake: Omitting relevant certifications or specialty training
commonMistakes.fix: Include certifications like NBCOT, AOTA specialty certifications, or training in sensory integration, hand therapy, or low-vision rehab—they're often ATS keywords.

commonMistakes.mistake: Generic references to 'patient care' without clinical depth
commonMistakes.fix: Replace vague language with specific assessments used (Functional Independence Measure, Canadian Occupational Performance Measure, Sensory Profile) and measurable functional improvements.

structureTips: Lead with your clinical license and any specialty certifications in the header or summary—hiring managers scan for 'OTR/L' and state license status immediately to confirm compliance.
Place your most recent and highest-acuity clinical setting first; if you're applying to schools, move any school-based experience or pediatric training to the top of your work history.

Use a 'Core Competencies' or 'Key Skills' section to mirror job posting language (e.g., 'ADL training,' 'mental health intervention,' 'EHR documentation')—this helps with both ATS scanning and human readers.

In the experience section, group bullets by outcome category: Assessment & Diagnosis, Intervention & Treatment, Patient/Caregiver Education, and Collaboration—it mirrors the workflow and shows organizational thinking.
salarySignal: Entry-level occupational therapists in the US typically earn $58,000–$68,000 annually; experienced OTs in private practice, specialty clinics, or management roles often earn $75,000–$95,000+. Salary varies by location, setting (hospital vs. school vs. private), and specialization.

faq.q: Should I list my NBCOT certification number on my resume?
faq.a: Yes, include 'NBCOT Certified' or 'NBCOT #[number]' near your license designation. It's an ATS keyword and proof you passed the national board exam. Some employers also require it for credentialing.

faq.q: How do I show experience if I'm a new graduate with only internships?
faq.a: Rename 'Internship' or 'Fieldwork' with the setting and your title, e.g., 'Clinical Occupational Therapist (Internship), Acute Care Hospital.' Use the same outcome-focused bullet format; hiring managers understand new grads, but they want to see you took your education seriously.

faq.q: What if I've worked in multiple OT settings (hospital, school, private practice)?
faq.a: List each setting separately in your work history and highlight what's unique about each (hospital: acute care, fast turnover; school: IEP writing, classroom integration; private: long-term relationships, specialized populations). This shows adaptability and breadth.

faq.q: Should I mention EHR systems I'm proficient in?
faq.a: Absolutely. Add a 'Technical Skills' section listing systems like Epic, Cerner, NextGen, or specialized OT software (e.g., Therapy Insights). Many job postings filter for specific EHR experience, so naming them is both ATS-friendly and a hiring advantage.

faq.q: How specific should I be about patient populations and diagnoses?
faq.a: Be specific: name diagnosis groups (stroke, spinal cord injury, autism, dementia, hand injuries) and client ages (pediatric, geriatric, adult). Employers want to know if you've worked with their target population; it's far more relevant than generic 'patient care' language.
